revision.text:
[[ja:双脚輪状文]] [[File:井辺八幡山古墳出土 双脚輪状文埴輪.JPG|thumb|250px|Paired-leg ring-patterned haniwa
Excavated from {{ill|Ibe Hachimanyama Kofun|ja|井辺八幡山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ibe Hachimanyama Kofun|1=Inbe Hachiman-yama Kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11373771}}, Wakayama Prefecture.]] '''Sōkyaku rinjōmon''' (双脚輪状文) refers to one of the decorative motifs of the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, appearing in the wall paintings of {{ill|Decorated kofun|en|Decorated kofun|qid=Q11626716|lt=Decorated kofun|1=Decorated kofun}} and as motifs on {{ill|figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Representational Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/figurative haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}. It features a distinctive shape, and while various theories about its origins and meanings have been proposed, no definitive conclusion has been reached{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=7-8|qq=}}. == Overview == [[File:大日山35号墳出土 双脚輪状文形冠帽をかぶった人物埴輪.JPG|thumb|200px|Haniwa figure wearing a paired-leg ring-patterned crown
Excavated from {{ill|Dainichiyama Tomb No. 35|ja|大日山35号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainichiyama Tomb No. 35|qq=}}, Wakayama Prefecture.]] [[File:双脚輪状文・釜尾古墳.svg|200px|thumb|Paired-leg ring-pattern wall painting
From {{ill|Kamao Kofun|en|Kamao Kofun|qid=Q24866261|lt=Kamao Kofun|1=Kamao Kofun}}, Kumamoto Prefecture.]] The paired-leg ring pattern consists of a circular disk with a central hole, typically featuring multiple small radial projections. Two longer projections (the "paired legs") extend outward in a crescent-shaped arc. Theories about its origin include influences from {{ill|Harpago chiragra|en|Harpago chiragra|qid=Q1257283|lt=spiny oyster shell bracelets|1=Harpago chiragra}}, feathered fans (sashiwa), lotus motifs, or flag-like objects{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=7-8|qq=}}. This motif was used in wall paintings and haniwa figurines from the early to late 6th century. Wall paintings featuring the motif were mainly found in Kyūshū, while paired-leg ring-pattern haniwa were primarily discovered in Honshū{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=55-62|qq=}}. The haniwa can be divided into two types: disk-type paired-leg ring-pattern haniwa, where the motif is mounted on a cylindrical haniwa, and crown-type paired-leg ring-pattern haniwa, where a crown featuring the motif adorns a figurative haniwa{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=63-78|qq=}}. According to Kato Shunpei, paired-leg ring patterns can be categorized into three types: wall-painting paired-leg ring patterns, Western Japan-style haniwa motifs, and Eastern Japan-style haniwa motifs. The wall paintings appeared first, with the earliest examples in {{ill|Kamao Kofun|en|Kamao Kofun|qid=Q24866261|lt=Kamao Kofun|1=Kamao Kofun}} spreading through central and northern Kyūshū in the early 6th century. From there, the motif spread to Western Japan, passing through sites such as {{ill|Ōtaniyama Tomb No. 22|ja|大谷山22号墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtaniyama Tomb No. 22|qq=}} in Wakayama. By the mid-6th century, it had reached the Kanto region, including sites like {{ill|Nakafutago Kofun|en|Nakafutago Kofun|qid=Q17229473|lt=Nakanifuko Kofun|1=Nakafutago Kofun}} in Gunma Prefecture{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=55-62|qq=}}. === Kyūshū Wall-Painting Paired-Leg Ring Patterns === The motif is mainly found in wall paintings within the stone chambers of horizontal burial tombs. Examples include {{ill|Kamao Kofun|en|Kamao Kofun|qid=Q24866261|lt=Kamao Kofun|1=Kamao Kofun}}, {{ill|Yokoyama Kofun|ja|横山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun|en|Ōzuka Kofun|qid=Q11572952|lt=Ōzuka Kofun|1=Ōzuka Kofun}}, {{ill|Kōkadani Kofun|lt=Kōkadani Kofun|lt=Kōkatani Kofun|ja|弘化谷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōkatani Kofun|qq=}}, and {{ill|Oni-no-iwa Kofun|ja|鬼ノ岩屋古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oni-no-iwa Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼の岩屋古墳}}. A total of 15 examples have been identified. Kato suggests these sites fall within the territory of the Fire (Hi) clan. The paintings feature vibrant colors, including red, yellow, white, black, green, and blue{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=55-62|qq=}}. === Western Japan-Style Paired-Leg Ring-Pattern Haniwa === Thirteen examples have been identified, with six found in the {{ill|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|en|Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11475228|lt=Iwashimizu Kofun Cluster|1=Iwase-Senzuka Kofun Cluster}} in Wakayama Prefecture. Other examples include sites such as {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun|en|Tsukiyama Kofun|qid=Q11603501|lt=Tsukiyama Kofun|1=Tsukiyama Kofun}}, Shinjō Tomb No. 36 in Ehime Prefecture, Kōmonyama Tomb No. 1 in Kagawa Prefecture, Shinai Kofun in Hyōgo Prefecture, Otonoridani Kofun in Kyoto Prefecture, and Aramaki Kofun in Nara Prefecture. The distribution suggests the influence of the {{ill|Ki Clan|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki Clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. Western Japan-style motifs typically have well-defined paired legs and a crescent-like shape. Crown-type paired-leg ring-pattern haniwa are limited to two examples from Ōtaniyama Tomb No. 22, while the rest are disk-type motifs mounted horizontally{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=63-78|qq=}}. === Eastern Japan-Style Paired-Leg Ring-Pattern Haniwa === Eastern Japan examples are found in the middlebasins of the Tone and Arakawa Rivers (in Gunma and Saitama Prefectures). Kato associates this distribution with the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}} clan. Of the examples, 34 are disk-type and 10 are crown-type haniwa. Eastern Japan-style motifs are simpler in design, with paired legs sometimes indistinct. Disk motifs were mounted both horizontally and vertically, reflecting a derivative of the Western Japan style{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=81-100|qq=}}. === Northern Japan Example === Kato identified a paired-leg ring-patterned fan haniwa from Kamiya-saku Tomb No. 101 in Fukushima Prefecture. He theorized it reached the region directly from Kyūshū, brought by the {{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|qid=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}}{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=101-119|qq=}}. == Research History == [[File:松林山古墳出土 貝釧.JPG|thumb|Spiny oyster shell bracelet
Excavated from {{ill|Shōrinzan Kofun|lt=Shōrinzan Kofun|lt=Matsubayama Kofun|ja|松林山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsubayama Kofun|qq=}}.]] [[File:Periodo kofun, haniwa, ventaglio cerimoniale, VI sec.JPG|thumb|100px|Fan-shaped haniwa
Theory posits this fan as the origin of the paired-leg ring pattern. Excavated from {{ill|Hongo Bessho, Fujioka, Gunma|ja|藤岡市本郷別所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hongo Bessho, Fujioka, Gunma|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.]] {{ill|Kōsaku Hamada|en|Kōsaku Hamada|qid=Q366007|lt=Hamada Kōsaku|1=Kōsaku Hamada}} and Umehara Suetoshi described the wall painting at {{ill|Kamao Kofun|en|Kamao Kofun|qid=Q24866261|lt=Kamao Kofun|1=Kamao Kofun}} in {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University Archaeological Reports, Volume 3|ja|京都帝国大学文学部考古学研究報告 第3冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University Archaeological Reports, Volume 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (1919). They referred to it as "a special pattern composed of a circle with radiating projections, fern-shaped protrusions, and curves surrounding the outer frame." They proposed three hypotheses regarding its origin: imitation of animals like {{ill|Starfish|en|Starfish|qid=Q25349|lt=starfish|1=Starfish}}, reptiles, or mammals; human eyes; or a combination of decorative motifs with radial projections{{sfn|Hamada Kōsaku|Umehara Suetoshi|1919|pp=9-13|qq=}}{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=18-19|qq=}}. In 1940, Umehara Suetoshi and {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} used the term "paired-leg ring pattern" for the first time in {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University Archaeological Reports, Volume 15|ja|京都帝国大学文学部考古学研究報告 第15冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University Archaeological Reports, Volume 15|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. They described a wall painting at {{ill|Ōzuka Kofun|en|Ōzuka Kofun|qid=Q11572952|lt=Ōzuka Kofun|1=Ōzuka Kofun}}, Fukuoka Prefecture, as "a circular motif with radiating projections and fern-shaped appendages." They speculated on its origin as a {{ill|Shellfish|ja|貝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shellfish|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Molluscan shellfish|qid=Q17198542}} or jellyfish while suggesting a ritualistic purpose for the pattern{{sfn|Umehara Suetoshi|Kobayashi Yukio|1940|p=30|qq=}}{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=18-19|qq=}}. In 1950, Kumagai Nobuo proposed that the paired-leg ring pattern in Ōtsuka Kofun was derived from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Koguryo|1=Goguryeo}} lotus patterns that had been simplified and altered. He suggested it functioned as a ritualistic symbol{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=19-22|qq=}}. {{ill|Saitō Tadashi|ja|斎藤忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitō Tadashi|1=Tadashi Saitō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11500562}} avoided definitive conclusions in his 1952 work, {{ill|Studies on Decorated Kofun|ja|装飾古墳の研究|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Studies on Decorated Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, suggesting the pattern was an imaginative and magnified design derived from a motif, imbued with mystical and ritual significance{{sfn|Saitō Tadashi|1952|pp=128-137|qq=}}{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=19-22|qq=}}. In 1956, {{ill|Higuchi Takayasu|ja|樋口隆康|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higuchi Takayasu|1=Takayasu Higuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3135463}} pointed out the similarity between paired-leg ring-pattern haniwa excavated from Kōmonyama Kofun in Kagawa Prefecture and the wall painting at Kamao Kofun. He connected these to fan-shaped haniwa from Gunma Prefecture, suggesting the origin lay in {{ill|fans (sashiwa)|ja|翳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fans (sashiwa)|1=Yi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q8364380}}{{sfn|Higuchi Takayasu|1956|pp=22-23|qq=}}{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=19-22|qq=}}. However, researchers like Saitō Tadashi and {{ill|Oda Fujio|ja|小田富士雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oda Fujio|1=Fujio Oda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q16265026}} critiqued this theory{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=19-22|qq=}}. {{ill|Usa Shin'ichi|ja|宇佐晋一|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usa Shin'ichi|1=Shin'ichi Usa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11449048}} and Nishitani Tadashi suggested in 1959 that the wall painting at Kamao Kofun, particularly the design on the left front wall, was the oldest form. They noted similarities between the six radiating projections and spiny oyster shell bracelets{{sfn|Usa Shin'ichi|Nishitani Tadashi|1959|pp=1-9|qq=}}{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=19-22|qq=}}. In 1964, Kobayashi Yukio categorized prior hypotheses into geometric patterns, depictions of objects, and forms inspired by flora, fauna, or human features. He pointed out weaknesses in the theory that the design represented an object, due to its depiction in both horizontal and vertical orientations{{sfn|Kobayashi Yukio|1964|pp=20-21|qq=}}{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=19-22|qq=}}. Fujii Isao and Ishiyama Isao theorized in 1979 that paired-leg ring-pattern haniwa served as secondary sources for wall paintings{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=19-22|qq=}}. In 1991, Wakamatsu Ryōichi associated the motif with {{ill|lotus patterns|ja|蓮華文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lotus patterns|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} brought to Japan by local clans after the Yamato court's military campaigns in Korea. He divided the haniwa into Kansai and Kantō types, with the former more faithfully replicating the lotus patterns, while the latter had simplified, decorative forms{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=22-26|qq=}}. Tatsumi Kazuhiro connected the motif to spiny oyster shell bracelets in 1992, interpreting it as a magical tool influenced by southern island customs{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=22-26|qq=}}. Takashima Katsuhisa classified the haniwa into two types in 2005, linking early forms to fan-shaped wooden artifacts and later forms to Kyūshū wall paintings{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=22-26|qq=}}. Hashiguchi Tatsuya identified a connection between the motif and bracelets from {{ill|Sinustrombus latissimus|en|Sinustrombus latissimus|qid=Q7525218|lt=Sinustrombus latissimus|1=Sinustrombus latissimus}} shells, emphasizing its roots in Yayoi-era magical traditions{{sfn|Hashiguchi Tatsuya|2004|pp=87-89|qq=}}{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=22-26|qq=}}. In 1999, Inamura Shigeru categorized haniwa into three types, describing circular paired-leg ring-pattern haniwa as the prototype{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=22-26|qq=}}. Fukushima Masayoshi theorized in 1999 that Ōtsuka Kofun wall paintings depicted military formations, identifying the paired-leg ring pattern as a flag design{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=22-26|qq=}}. In 2018, Kato Shunpei suggested the motif originated from spiny oyster shell bracelets, created by the {{ill|Fire Clan (Hi no Kimi)|ja|火君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fire Clan (Hi no Kimi)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and disseminated through ancient clan interactions{{sfn|Kato|2018|pp=197-199|qq=}}. == References == {{Reflist|2|qq=}} == See Also == * {{ill|Decorated kofun|en|Decorated kofun|qid=Q11626716|lt=Decorated kofun|1=Decorated kofun}} * {{ill|Figurative haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Representational Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Figurative haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}} * {{ill|Harpago chiragra|en|Harpago chiragra|qid=Q1257283|lt=Spiny oyster shell bracelet|1=Harpago chiragra}} {{Kofun navbar|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|双脚輪状文|version=94497016|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:双脚輪状文]] {{wikidata link|Q117086855}} [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Haniwa]] [[Category:Visual motifs]] [[Category:Decorated kofun]]
